ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for vegetable tempura; A0FKP (battered/fried vegetables) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for vegetable tempura as a specific food concept.

main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, egg, cold water)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetable tempura consists of approximately 5–6 pieces of assorted battered and deep-fried vegetables, weighing around 150 g.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Tempura — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetable Tempura (F14080061). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetable tempura exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous battered and deep-fried vegetable preparations. The wheat-flour batter raises GI above raw vegetables (GI ~15–20) but the fat content (16.9 g/100g) significantly slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption, placing the composite dish in the low-to-medium boundary. GL per 150 g serving calculated as: (45 × 22.45 × 1.5) / 100 = 15.2. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this specific preparation is not available in approved sources.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content at 67 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Vegetable tempura is low in sodium (67 mg/100g) and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, provided the tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu) is avoided or used sparingly, as it contributes significant additional sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (22.45 g/100g) with wheat-flour batter and deep-fried preparation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The wheat-flour batter and deep-frying process elevate the glycaemic impact of this dish; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 2–3 pieces, avoid the dipping sauce, and pair with high-fibre foods to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat content (16.94 g/100g) from deep-frying and caloric density (255 kcal/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high fat content from deep-frying contributes to elevated caloric density; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency and portion size, and prefer steamed or grilled vegetable preparations as a lower-fat alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; steamed or roasted vegetable preparations are strongly preferred, and nightshade vegetables (e.g., eggplant) should be monitored for individual tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable tempura (野菜の天ぷら, yasai no tempura) is a classic Japanese dish consisting of assorted vegetables — commonly including sweet potato, pumpkin, eggplant, green beans, lotus root, and shishito pepper — coated in a light, airy wheat-flour-and-egg batter and deep-fried in oil at high temperature. The batter is intentionally thin and minimally mixed to preserve a delicate, crispy texture that contrasts with the tender, lightly cooked vegetables inside. The dish is typically served with tentsuyu dipping sauce (a blend of dashi, mirin, and soy sauce) and grated daikon radish. Nutritionally, vegetable tempura provides 255 kcal per 100g, with 16.94g fat, 22.45g carbohydrates, 3.17g protein, 2.37g dietary fibre, and only 67mg sodium. The high fat content from frying oil contributes to caloric density and slows gastric emptying. Clinically, the dish is low in sodium and suitable for hypertension management when consumed without dipping sauce, but the battered deep-fried preparation raises glycaemic impact and is a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), making this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Vegetable tempura is vegetarian but not vegan due to egg in the batter, and contains gluten from wheat flour.
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable tempura?
Is vegetable tempura suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of vegetable tempura?
野菜の天ぷら カロリー
meal_context: Side dish in a Japanese set meal (teishoku)

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14080061) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented as such. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for vegetable tempura (USDA SR Legacy #35181) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vegetable tempura (NDB #35181) as the HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is an adapted value. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific vegetable tempura preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the level of precision required. 3. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for vegetable tempura exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. Dietary tag 'low_sodium' from input is retained as sodium is 67 mg/100g, which is below the Singapore HPB low-sodium threshold of 120 mg/100g. 5. Vegan flag is false due to egg in tempura batter. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) does not apply to this record.
